content.assessment: This proposal most directly engages network-planning levers (zoning, commuting, maintainer exemptions) as a distinct causal channel from admissions design, but its implementation is constrained by the same centralized maintenance structure and legal exemption precedents that limit local network adaptation.
opportunities: Zoning and commuting-restriction levers could reduce inter-school inequality without requiring new track-age restructuring or capacity expansion
strengths: Directly targets geographic/network drivers of stratification (village-to-vocational funneling, commuting patterns) rather than only admissions design
Legal lever (review of anti-discrimination maintainer exemptions) is a network-governance tool distinct from admissions reform
threats: 2015 Curia exemption ruling for Church-maintained schools creates a legal precedent risk that could block or reverse zoning-based desegregation efforts
Demographic contraction combined with centralized control limits local capacity to redraw zones or adjust transport patterns responsively
weaknesses: Centralization under KLIK curtails municipal and institutional autonomy over network planning, constraining the very zoning/local-adaptation tools this proposal depends on
Prior administrative desegregation rules of the 2000s produced only temporary reductions before stagnating, suggesting network-lever durability is historically weak
